one_line_takeaway: A broad, taxonomy-driven survey mapping how Transformer and LLM architectures (attention, CNN/ViT hybrids, GAN-Transformers, BERT/GPT) are applied to intrusion detection across networks, IoT, vehicles, and critical infrastructure, useful as a landscape map but offering compiled, non-comparable benchmark numbers rather than independent evidence.
key_finding: Transformers and LLMs consistently outperform prior RNN/LSTM- and classical-ML-based IDS across the surveyed literature by using self-attention to capture long-range temporal/contextual dependencies in network flows, logs, and packet payloads, and encoder-only (BERT) vs decoder-only (GPT) LLMs offer complementary strengths (bidirectional context vs generative next-token modeling). The survey documents near-saturated reported accuracy/F1 (frequently 97-100%) on standard IDS benchmarks and shows Transformer/LLM-IDS is a fast-growing field (publications surging from 2022). It stresses that reported numbers come from heterogeneous datasets and settings and highlights recurring open problems: class imbalance (widely mitigated with SMOTE/CGAN augmentation), high computational cost limiting real-time/resource-constrained (IoT) deployment, poor generalization/domain adaptation, and weak interpretability.
core_contribution: A single-author (Hamza Kheddar, University of Medea, Algeria) comprehensive SURVEY of Transformer- and LLM-based intrusion detection systems (IDS). Its own contribution is synthesis and taxonomy, not new experiments: (1) a bibliometric paper-selection methodology (Scopus-anchored query across IEEE/ScienceDirect/Wiley/Springer + arXiv/SSRN/TechRxiv, English-only, 2017-2024 priority) yielding 118 Transformer/LLM-for-IDS papers out of 180 cited works; (2) a comparison against six prior cybersecurity-LLM surveys (Table 1) arguing this is the first to focus specifically and comprehensively on IDS, reviewing 103 Transformer/LLM-based IDS papers versus 0-17 in the others; (3) an architecture taxonomy of Transformer variants for IDS (attention types, CNN/LSTM-Transformer hybrids, ViT, GAN-Transformer, and encoder-only/decoder-only/encoder-decoder LLMs such as BERT and GPT families); (4) a catalog of IDS datasets, attack categories, preprocessing pipelines, and evaluation metrics; (5) a mapping of deployment environments (computer networks, IoT/IIoT, critical infrastructure, cloud/SDN, autonomous vehicles/UAV); and (6) research challenges and future directions (interpretability, scalability, adaptability). It is organized around five/six research questions (RQ1-RQ6).
threat_model: As a defensive-IDS survey, the adversaries in scope are the attacks the reviewed detectors defend against: DoS/DDoS (TCP/UDP flooding, ICMP Smurf, SYN/ACK flood, HTTP Slowloris, DNS amplification), remote-to-local (R2L), user-to-root/privilege escalation (U2R), PROBE/scanning, malware and encrypted-malicious traffic, phishing, MitM, APTs, web attacks (SQL injection, XSS, RCE), and in-vehicle CAN-bus attacks (flooding, fuzzy, spoofing, replay). It distinguishes signature-based detection (known attacks) from anomaly-based detection (zero-day). The survey also surfaces the DUAL-USE / offensive side of LLMs as a threat: it reviews LLM tools that generate attack payloads or automate offense (GPTFuzzer producing WAF-evading SQLi/XSS/RCE payloads, PentestGPT, LLM-driven Linux privilege-escalation guidance, ChatGPT/Bard generating malicious payloads), plus adversarial robustness (the Fooling Rate metric).
limitations: (1) It is a survey with no original empirical validation, so its evidentiary weight rests entirely on the primary studies it aggregates. (2) The tabulated accuracy/F1 figures are drawn from different datasets, splits, and threat scopes and are NOT directly comparable; the survey tabulates 'best result / max improvement' per paper without normalization or meta-analysis, so head-to-head model rankings cannot be inferred from Table 5. (3) Single-author, English-only, database-restricted scope, with heavy reliance on preprints (40 of 180 cited works), risking selection and hype bias toward inflated positive results. (4) Many reported >99% scores likely reflect over-optimistic benchmark conditions rather than deployment performance, a caveat the survey acknowledges only qualitatively. (5) GROUNDING CAVEAT: only the first 22 of 38 pages were extracted, so the detailed Challenges (Sec 7) and Conclusion (Sec 8) were reviewed from the introduction's stated scope rather than their full text.

datasets_used: NSL-KDD (reviewed works, e.g. FL-MHA Acc=99.45%, Denseformer 85.65%)
CICIDS-2017 / CIC IDS2017 (DE-VIT 99.5%, ConViTML 99.75%, ViT Acc=98.5%, GAN-Transformer Acc=97.24%)
Car-Hacking / In-Vehicle / OTIDS (CAN-BERT F1=81-99%; CANBERT OTIDS Acc=100%; Self-Attention Car Hacking F1=99.47%)
X-IIoTID, WUSTL-IIoT-2021, EdgeIIoT, TON_IoT, NF-ToN-IoT-V2 (GAN-BERT F1=98.799%)
CSIC 2010, syscall_args, Web server log, KDDCup99, ISCX2012, AWID, NVD, ECU-IoHT, SMD, 5GC PFCP, MalwSpecSys
quantitative_results: Corpus: 118 Transformer/LLM-for-IDS papers selected; 180 total cited works (40 preprints); publication surge from 2022; the survey reviews 103 Transformer/LLM-based IDS papers (NRTLIP) vs 0-17 for the six compared prior surveys
Field distribution of the 180 references: 65.55% Transformer-and-LLM for IDS, 16.65% surveys/reviews, 10.55% other, 3.88% LLM-only, 3.37% IDS-only

Reviewed-work highlights (from Table 5, compiled not original): Bi-GPT CAN AUC=99.8% (+0.3 vs Uni-GPT); FL-MHA NSL-KDD Acc=99.45% (+3.08); CANBERT OTIDS Acc=100%; GAN-BERT NF-ToN-IoT-V2 F1=98.799% (+13.779); LAnoBERT Thunderbird F1=99.90%; GPT-Neo web-server-log F1=99.25% (+2.92); MHA MQTTset Acc=99.56% (+10)

Reviewed LLM/ViT works (in-text): DoLLM zero-shot DDoS F1=96.4%; DE-VIT CIC-IDS2017 Acc=99.5%; ConViTML Acc=99.75%; VINCENT best Acc=87.2%; Denseformer NSL-KDD Acc=85.65%; GPT vs BERT threat-intel Acc=61% (+17 over BERT); PentestGPT benchmark 13 scenarios / 182 subtasks; BT-TPF BERT-of-Theseus IoT model with only 788 parameters (~90% reduction)
baselines_compared: Six prior cybersecurity-LLM surveys [17-22] (Table 1) contrasted for coverage of background, bibliometrics, Transformer/LLM-based IDS, datasets, metrics, applications, gaps

Within reviewed works, Transformer/LLM detectors are benchmarked against RNN/LSTM, CNN-LSTM, BiLSTM, DNN/DAGMM, decision tree, XGBoost/LightGBM/CatBoost, ResNet, GIDS, DeepFed, TadGAN, NeuTraL AD, and prior BERT-based log models (DeepLog, LogSy, NeuralLog)
